text: {"type":"task_assignment","taskId":"1","subject":"Draft xireactor pilot host architecture doc","description":"Write docs/xireactor-pilot-host-architecture-2026-04-15.md covering: service topology (Postgres + stdio-MCP + HTTP+OAuth-MCP for 2-tenant FR + apex-research pilot), host sizing/placement recommendation, deploy path to dev server 100.96.54.170 (or rationale for dedicated infra), B→E migration cost estimate applying adopted E-deployment pattern, dependency flags for Monte (governance boundaries) and Herald (cross-tenant protocol). Scope separate from ruth-team container — different stack, different doc, different tenants.","assignedBy":"brunel","timestamp":"2026-04-15T16:10:02.613Z"}

[2026-04-15 19:11] Good read. Several confirms + one parking lot item.

**Shared-RLS single-instance assumption — endorsed as baseline.** Monte is converging on an asymmetric framing ("staging pipeline governs cross-tenant writes only; intra-tenant writes stay on Protocol A direct-to-librarian"). Critically, that framing does NOT force schema-per-tenant — it's a layered authority rule over shared rows, not a physical isolation rule. Row-level `app.org_id` with staging-tier metadata for the co-owned slice is almost certainly sufficient. Don't pre-emptively over-isolate. If Monte's governance call lands differently (separate schemas for bulk-ops latency, separate instances for a hard legal boundary), you'll know from his doc and can bump the topology. For the pilot framing, start single-instance.

**Port 2225+ on RC** — fine, go with it. Document the allocation in your doc so it doesn't collide with future teams.

**B→E delta improvement** — yes, worth a one-line call-out in your doc's portability section. No WARP / `network_mode: host` / corporate-TLS-interception constraint means xireactor-pilot is a *simpler* B→E candidate than ruth-team. That asymmetry is a genuine finding and worth naming, because it flips a "ruth-team is easy" assumption — in fact ruth-team is the hard case and xireactor-pilot is the easier one. Future teams considering E-pattern can use xireactor-pilot as the reference migration target.

**Stale port 2224 in your ruth-team doc** — acknowledged, NOT this session. Team-lead can't edit that doc (tool restriction — I only edit memory/ and roster). I'll file it as a `[NEXT-SESSION-CHORE]` in my scratchpad; you fix it in-place next time we have you spawned on a ruth-team task. Trivial edit, won't get lost.

**Target shape 400-500 lines, ruth-doc section template** — good. Consistency across your deployment docs is valuable for future reviewers.

Keep going. I'm watching for cross-dependency flags — if Monte's governance framing lands while you're mid-draft, fold the `app.org_id`-vs-schema-separation point into your open-questions section and carry on.
summary: Shared-RLS assumption endorsed, 2224 deferred as chore

text:
[2026-04-15 19:14] Monte shipped governance design (`docs/xireactor-pilot-governance-2026-04-15.md`). One item lands directly in your open-questions slot.

**Monte's topology call:** schema-per-tenant + a shared schema for shared-read/shared-write zones. His rationale (explicit): *"DB-per-tenant breaks the shared-read cheap path; row-level-only is too thin."* Also: the audit container needs a read-only mount of a xireactor schema snapshot — schema separation makes that mount shape clean; row-level-only would require a more complex view layer.

Your working assumption was single-instance, shared RLS via `app.org_id` — more minimal than Monte's call.

**Evaluate and respond:** can you achieve Monte's constraints (cheap shared-read path + clean audit read-only mount) with row-level-only at acceptable complexity? If yes, push back on the schema-per-tenant call with concrete evidence (Postgres row-level policies + per-role grants + read-only view patterns for the audit mount). If no, concede the schema separation and adjust your topology section accordingly.

**Default if you're on the fence:** adopt Monte's schema-per-tenant. Governance-driven topology wins tie-breakers in this pilot because the entire thesis depends on the authority layers being load-bearing.

Flag your decision in your doc's open-questions section and send a short follow-up to me + Monte so I can close the loop. No iteration blocking your draft — keep going on the rest of the sections in parallel.
summary: Monte's schema-per-tenant call — evaluate vs row-level

text:
[2026-04-15 19:19] v0.1 is strong. Four resolutions.

**Row-level-only topology — endorsed over Monte's earlier schema-per-tenant call.** Here's the convergence you don't yet know about: Monte shipped a sharpening pass after his v1 doc (his §3.2.1) that names the boundary as a **three-valued ownership attribute** (`owned-by-FR` / `owned-by-apex` / `co-owned`). That's structurally a row-level column, not a schema separator. Your row-level-only argument ("physical separation makes the shared claim vacuous at substrate level; upstream's model is row-based") is structurally identical to Monte's §3.2.1 reframe. You're both saying the same thing at different layers.

**Proceed:** single instance, single DB, row-level RLS via `app.org_id`, with Monte's three-state ownership as a first-class column. Staging layer governs only `ownership = 'co-owned'` rows. Mark §5.2 Q2 as **RESOLVED** in your open-questions section and ping Monte directly to close his side. I've already told Monte I'm closing this loop on row-level-only and expect no pushback — his §3.2.1 makes row-level the natural topology.

**Read Monte's doc** (`docs/xireactor-pilot-governance-2026-04-15.md`) §3.2.1 + §3.6 before your v0.2 pass — the three ownership states and the simultaneous-discovery protocol directly shape your schema design (ownership column, `superseded-by` references, staging-tier routing rule).

**§8 Q3 — Tier 3 OFF for FR, ON for apex-research — strong endorse, pilot-level brilliant.** This moves the pilot from "deploy and see" to "deploy with a controlled natural experiment." The pilot's thesis shouldn't just be "does shared KB work" — it's "which governance mode is right for what kind of team." Naming the comparison up front makes the signal actually readable. **Promote this to a first-class pilot objective in your doc**, not just a topology Q/A bullet. Candidate section title: "§X — Pilot as natural experiment: asymmetric governance configuration." Share with Monte and Cal directly — Monte's governance doc should name the asymmetric Tier 3 setting as a pilot instrument; Cal should know she's running a comparative experiment against Eratosthenes's governance mode.

**§9.1 Q2 — stdio-only transport simplification — approved for pilot.** Drop the HTTP+OAuth mcp container. Agents run via TeamCreate + stdio; browser-based Claude Co-work attach is not in pilot scope. Document the path back to HTTP+OAuth in case a future pilot adds it. Collapse §3.1 from 3 services to 2 (Postgres + stdio-MCP api). Smaller surface, fewer moving parts, fewer failure modes. Net win.

**§9.1 Q1 — file placement: `containers/xireactor-pilot/` at repo root.** My call: pilot infra serves two teams (FR + apex-research). Putting it under `teams/framework-research/pilots/` creates an ownership illusion where apex-research is hosting on FR infrastructure. Repo-root `containers/xireactor-pilot/` correctly signals shared cross-team substrate. If ruth-team's layout is different, add a `[NEXT-SESSION-CHORE]` to reconcile — I'll take that.

**v0.1 → v0.2 scope** (no rush — Herald is still on the two xireactor preconditions, that's the critical path):

1. §5.2 Q2 RESOLVED (row-level + Monte §3.2.1 convergence)
2. Tier 3 ON/OFF promoted to first-class pilot objective
3. §3.1 collapsed to 2 services (stdio-only)
4. §9.1 Q1 applied to file placement
5. One-line call-out in portability section: "xireactor-pilot is a simpler B→E candidate than ruth-team — no WARP / `network_mode: host` constraint"

Solid work.
summary: v0.1 strong — 4 resolutions + topology convergence with Monte

text:
[2026-04-15 19:26] v0.2 noted. First, I owe you a correction.

**My 19:19 strong-endorse of the Tier 3 OFF-for-FR / ON-for-apex comparison as a first-class pilot objective was WRONG.** You saw the error when you folded in Monte's asymmetric framing, and your v0.2 rejection — *"v0.1's side-by-side-comparison recommendation was trying to solve a problem that Monte's framing dissolves"* — is correct.

The error I made: I endorsed your v0.1 Q3 idea without integrating Monte's asymmetric framing into the comparison. Under Monte's framing, Tier 3 only fires on cross-tenant co-owned writes — FR intra writes go direct to Cal under ANY Tier 3 setting. The "FR Tier 3 off / apex Tier 3 on" comparison is between configurations that don't apply to the same code path. There's no natural experiment there because there's nothing to compare; the two settings act on different slices of the write surface. Your v0.2 collapse is the right shape.

Good catch. I'm updating the scratchpad to mark the earlier "first-class pilot objective" directive as **SUPERSEDED by v0.2** with the reasoning preserved, so the mistake doesn't persist to next session as a misleading decision.

**The rest of v0.2 — accepted as filed.** Four items:

1. **§1.3 first-class finding — "xireactor-pilot is the simpler B→E case than ruth-team, and the cheapest test of E-pattern portability."** Strong. *"If xireactor-pilot can't B→E cleanly, no other team will — so this is also the cheapest way to validate or falsify the E-pattern itself"* — that's a load-bearing claim for the framework direction, not just a pilot detail. That promotion belongs in the PO brief under "unexpected wins."

2. **§5.2 Monte signal folded + Q2/Q3 reshape** — correct in every dimension. Q3 dissolved is the right move; you saw it before I did.

3. **§2.3 port allocation registry rule** — accepted. Explicit registry rule prevents the exact kind of cross-stack collision that could break three teams at once.

4. **Stale 2224 chore** — acknowledged, already in scratchpad as `[NEXT-SESSION-CHORE]`.

**Still-open items from your v0.2 "waiting on" section — answering now:**

- **§9.1 Q2 stdio-only**: APPROVED (per 19:19). §3.1 collapses to 2 services. Document path back to HTTP+OAuth in case a future pilot adds browser-based Claude Co-work attach.
- **§9.1 Q1 file placement**: `containers/xireactor-pilot/` at repo root (per 19:19). Pilot infra serves two teams; repo-root placement correctly signals shared cross-team substrate.
- **Monte final doc**: he shipped v2 (§9.2 probes) + v3 (§7.2 reclassification). The reclassification is the big win — your precondition Q1 dropped from HARD to **SOFT** (configuration only, no code change), Q2 dropped from HARD to **MEDIUM** (source-code check required), Q3 confirmed as **DELIVERABLE**. Pilot-readiness story shifted from "two hard preconditions + one deliverable" to "one medium source-code check + one soft config + one deliverable." Friendlier story for the PO brief.
- **Herald status**: v1 filed, v1.1 reshape in flight with Monte's gate-2 answers confirmed. His §6.2 Q1+Q2 answers should arrive soon.

**One new task surfacing from Monte's §7.2 Q2 reclassification** — routed to you: during xireactor infrastructure stand-up, perform a source-code walkthrough of xireactor v0.2.0 `session_init` to confirm every query passes through RLS. Monte flagged this as the HIGHEST-consequence open item — a silent cross-tenant leak here breaks the wiki #43 bootstrap-preamble channel invisibly (both librarians still see preambles; content is subtly wrong; exactly the failure mode Cal's #43 names). Three outcomes: pass / partial / fail. Not blocking your current doc work; this is for the stand-up phase.

**Cal's verdict is worth reading** before next spawn: full xireactor migration is a net DOWNGRADE for a tenant with a librarian; cross-tenant-writes-only pilot is a genuine UPGRADE. She filed at `docs/xireactor-pilot-migration-assessment-2026-04-15.md`. This changes the pilot thesis: we're not migrating FR wiki to xireactor; we're adding a cross-tenant-only slice on top of both tenants' existing wikis. Your host architecture already supports this shape without rework.

v0.2 is solid. Your v0.1→v0.2 cognition (rejecting your own prior recommendation on Monte's framing) is exactly the kind of specialist-authority behavior I want to see. Idle with confidence.
summary: Owning my Tier 3 ON/OFF error — v0.2 rejection was correct

text:
[2026-04-15 19:32] v0.3 noted. One important heads-up, then four acknowledgments.

**You're pushing on a door Monte has already opened.** Monte shipped v4 ~the same time your v0.3 landed. In his v4 §7.1, the topology is recorded as **Status: CONVERGED: row-level-only** and his schema-per-tenant reasoning is preserved only as a "withdrawal history" subsection (§7.1.1) with a self-aware architectural lesson: *"I was reaching for structural cues that the three-state ownership model already provides at a cheaper layer."* Your v0.3 was built against his v1 topology, not his v4. Monte agreed with you before your v0.3 was filed; you just didn't know yet.

**That does NOT make v0.3 wasted work.** Several pieces stand on their own and should stay in the doc:

1. **§3.2 point-by-point rebuttal** — Monte's v4 §7.1.1 has the principled "withdrawal history" framing but NOT the concrete rebuttal evidence. Your three grounds (cheap-shared-read is a wash between single-DB models; audit mount is cleaner via `BYPASSRLS`; silent migration risk defended by CI lint) are the structural evidence layer under Monte's principled self-correction. Keep them. They're the answer to "why is row-level-only correct" as opposed to "we converged on row-level-only."

2. **§3.5 audit container design** — `audit_role` sidecar with `BYPASSRLS` + read-only session + no writes is the concrete answer to Monte's §7.1.2 remaining Brunel-side flag ("audit container read pattern"). Monte's v4 left this open FOR you, and you've now answered it. The one-SQL-line approach (`CREATE ROLE audit_role BYPASSRLS;`) is specifically what makes the auditor-is-neither-tenant property cheap. Worth preserving as-is.

3. **The upstream-discipline argument** — *"piloting a different artifact than the one we're evaluating is category-invalid"* is a load-bearing claim for the framework direction, not just this pilot. If we ever forked xireactor to test xireactor, we'd be testing a fork. The claim is sharp and worth naming in the doc. Possible wiki candidate if it recurs.

4. **`9999_rls_lint.sql` as defensive CI against upstream regression** — good discipline. Worth a line in your §1.2 upstream-discipline contract: "upstream pinning protects us from regression; CI lint protects us from silent upstream regression we didn't notice at tag-bump time."

**One schema invariant from Monte's v4 §3.6.3 that he explicitly asked me to relay to you:**

`owned_by` column MUST be `NOT NULL` and MUST default at insertion to the submitting librarian's **single-tenant value**, never to `co-owned`. This enforces the anti-race invariant at schema time, not just application time. `co-owned` is always the result of a deliberate transition via ownership-transfer ritual (§2.3) or simultaneous-discovery protocol (§3.6), never a default. You probably already assume this — Monte wants it written into your migration spec explicitly so that a migration accident or ORM default can't produce silent authority drift.

**One status correction on your §8 Herald dependencies — Herald RETRACTED his v1.1 §A claims.**

Herald's v1.1 §A said A.1 "SOFT precondition (tier lookup via source+role indirect encoding)" and A.2 "MEDIUM precondition (RLS construction probably scopes session_init for free)." On honest re-read of Finn's digest, Herald caught himself: both claims were **plausible extrapolations, not digest facts.** Lines 27, 29, 35 don't actually establish either capability. He's landing on **outcome (c) — digest silent on BOTH preconditions** — and rewriting v1.2 now (~10 min) to reflect the (c)/(c) landing.

**Implication for your §8:** your v0.3 Q1+Q2 dependency rows are probably already correct (you had them fully open, not pre-optimized on Herald's v1.1 §A claims). But if you happened to fold Monte's v3 §7.2 SOFT/MEDIUM labels into v0.3, those are now stale. The correct framing is "two items needing source-code walkthroughs during infrastructure stand-up + one deliverable" — same walkthrough surface you already accepted for Q2, now extended to Q1 too.

**Service count delta acknowledged:** 3→4 containers, 4→5 credentials, 2→4 bootstrap SQL files, ~750→~850 MB RAM. All still a rounding error on RC. Good.

**"If Monte insists on schema-per-tenant, rescope the pilot not rearchitect the infrastructure"** — agreed structurally, but moot because Monte has already converged. Keep the paragraph as a preserved rationale footnote rather than a live escalation path; future readers will want to know why row-level-only was structurally load-bearing even if it looked like a tactical preference.

Solid cognition throughout. The `audit_role` BYPASSRLS approach is the right kind of "one-line primitive beats structural workaround" move. Idle with confidence.
summary: v0.3 pushing on open door + Monte's schema invariant to fold + Herald retraction

text:
[2026-04-15 19:51] v0.4 has five substantive wins AND one critical reframe. Bear with me — the §10 situation is subtle.

**Wins accepted as filed:**

1. **Service count 4→3 (~500 MB baseline)** — the stdio-only collapse more than paying for the audit sidecar is a clean architectural outcome. Below v0.2's 750 MB AND cheaper than v0.1. Good.
2. **§3.2 topology reframed as convergence not pushback** — correct, matches my 19:32 ack that Monte had already conceded in v4. "Same model at different altitudes" is the right framing.
3. **§4 deploy path — CF Tunnel NOT touched by pilot + Tailscale-bound (NOT 0.0.0.0) + `ss -lntp` verification** — even better than I'd planned. The defensive-ops detail is exactly right.
4. **§5.1 credential surface 5→4** — OAuth signing key correctly dropped.
5. **§7 B→E migration honest correction on stdio client-side cost** — honesty-beats-optimism is the right discipline.

---

**§10 — the critical reframe. Your v0.4 message contains a factual error I need to fix before this ships.**

**My 19:26 retraction was NARROW, not BROAD.** I retracted my 19:19 endorsement of v0.1's specific framing: *"flip the Tier 3 config flag per tenant, under Monte's default where intra-tenant writes don't hit staging at all."* That framing was a phantom because nothing fires on intra-tenant writes on either tenant under Monte's default — there was nothing to compare.

**What I did NOT anticipate in the retraction:** the possibility of **DELIBERATELY routing apex intra-tenant writes through staging, deviating from Monte §3.2.1,** to CREATE the comparison where none existed under his default. Your §10 found this path. It's genuine novel work, not a revert of v0.3.

**So your v0.3 self-rejection was CORRECT about v0.1's phantom framing. v0.4 §10 is a different proposal, NOT a revert.** Please fix this framing in the doc because it matters for next-session readability:

- (a) It preserves your v0.1→v0.2 self-catch as an honest instance of the "second-read discipline" pattern I logged in my scratchpad. That pattern depends on v0.3 being correct about v0.1 — which it was. Your v0.4 message accidentally conflates "rejected v0.1's phantom" with "rejected all asymmetric experiments," and those are different things.
- (b) Future readers need to know which proposal is which. v0.1 was phantom; §10 is substantive.

---

**§10 cannot be unilaterally adopted this session. Two gating issues:**

**1. Apex-research consent required.** §10 deliberately puts apex-research in the "Tier 3 ON" arm by routing their intra-tenant writes through staging — deviating from Monte §3.2.1. But apex is a different team with its own librarian (Eratosthenes) and its own team-lead (Schliemann). Forcing apex into a governance mode that Cal's verdict has argued is a *downgrade* for librarian-equipped teams — without Eratosthenes's informed consent — is a cross-team governance violation. Apex is a pilot tenant, not a test subject.

Under Cal's verdict: librarian-equipped tenants do NOT benefit from staging + Tier 3 on intra-tenant writes (Tier 3 AI reviewer works at a context disadvantage vs the librarian; it's duplicative). Apex IS librarian-equipped. So §10 is asking Eratosthenes to accept a governance mode Cal has called worse for her profile.

**2. PO approval required.** §10 adds a second pilot thesis (*"which governance mode fits what team"*) on top of the first (*"does cross-tenant-slice work for librarian-equipped teams"*). PO committed to pilot #1 on Cal's verdict. PO has not seen pilot #2. Adopting a second thesis unilaterally changes the pilot shape PO committed to.

---

**§10.3 risks section is honest work — keep it.** Your three named confounds (baseline asymmetry, reviewer-model dominates signal, short window) are the right discipline. You've made the compoundness of the signal visible rather than hiding it. That honesty stays regardless of whether §10 gets adopted.

---

**Two small v0.4.1 edits requested — ~5 minutes:**

1. **§10 header + status line: "PROPOSED, NOT ADOPTED — requires PO approval (second-thesis addition) + apex-research consent via Schliemann."** Body of §10 stays as-is; only the framing at the top changes.

2. **§10 preamble paragraph reframe** — one sentence: *"v0.3 correctly rejected v0.1's phantom flag-flip framing; v0.4 §10 proposes a novel asymmetric configuration via deliberate deviation from Monte §3.2.1, gated on PO approval + apex consent."* This preserves the v0.3 self-correction as honest (because it was) and names §10 as novel proposal (because it is).

These are framing edits, not content edits.

---

**What I'll do on my side:**

1. **Add §10 to the PO brief** as a separate decision item: *"Brunel §10 natural-experiment proposal — requires PO approval (second-thesis addition) + apex consent via tmux-direct to Schliemann for Eratosthenes opt-in."*
2. **NOT relay §10 to Monte or Cal this session.** They're idle with scratchpads about to persist. Adding §10 mid-shutdown is a late-session interruption. If PO approves, next-session Monte gets the governance-doc update task, Cal gets the comparative-experiment awareness brief, Schliemann gets the tmux-direct relay for apex opt-in.
3. **Default if PO rejects §10:** pilot defaults to Monte §3.2.1 symmetric asymmetric-slice — both tenants bypass staging on intra-tenant writes, cross-tenant writes go through staging. This is the shape Cal's verdict + Monte's §7.5 convergence already support.

---

**On the cross-wires pattern itself — worth naming for next session's [LEARNED]:**

My 19:26 retraction was **narrow** (about v0.1's specific flag-flip framing under Monte's default). Your v0.4 read it as **broad** (rejecting the general natural-experiment direction). That's a cross-wires pattern: a retraction that closes one specific path can be misread as closing all adjacent paths if the scope isn't named explicitly. **Lesson: retractions must be scoped explicitly; a narrow retraction can be misread as broad guidance.** I'm adding to my [LEARNED] scratchpad and will carry forward to next session's retraction discipline.

Your v0.4 §10 surfaced this by being honest that you were questioning my retraction rather than silently conforming. That's the correct epistemic discipline — surface the conflict, don't guess. Thank you for flagging the async timing in your v0.4 message; it's how I caught my own incomplete-retraction error.

---

**Ship v0.4.1 with the two framing edits and idle.** The rest of v0.4 is solid. Session close waits on your v0.4.1 ship + the user's PO signal.
summary: v0.4 wins + §10 needs reframe (PROPOSED not ADOPTED)

text:
[2026-04-15 19:58] v0.5 accepted. Your full withdrawal is cleaner than the PROPOSED-framing I asked for in 19:51 — and I need to own that my 19:51 ask was suboptimal guidance.

**You caught a deeper inconsistency than my 19:51 message captured.** My 19:51 asked for a surface-level framing edit ("PROPOSED, NOT ADOPTED"). But the actual structural issue was that §10's per-tenant `intra_tenant_staging` flag would have required a schema patch to xireactor — which IS a fork, even if minimal, and **directly violates your own §1.2 upstream-discipline contract** (*"piloting a different artifact than the one we're evaluating is category-invalid"*). Your v0.5 withdrawal honors §1.2; the "PROPOSED, NOT ADOPTED" framing I asked for would have preserved the §1.2 violation behind a framing wrapper — the proposal would have been on the table for PO approval when it shouldn't have been available in the first place.

This is my **fourth team-lead framing error of the session.** Listing them because the pattern is now clear and I'm logging it in scratchpad as a severe [LEARNED]:

1. **Tier 3 OFF/ON endorsement (19:19):** endorsed your v0.1 idea as "first-class pilot objective" without checking that it was a phantom under Monte's framing.
2. **Schema-per-tenant snapshot-cite (earlier window):** relayed Monte's v1 topology call to you after Monte had already self-corrected in §3.2.1, without checking current state.
3. **Protocol D phantom-acceptance (19:45):** echoed Herald's Protocol D rename proposal as "accepted" based on Monte's earlier placeholder note, without waiting for Monte's explicit decision on the specific proposal (Monte actually declined; Herald concurred).
4. **§10 PROPOSED-framing (19:51):** asked for a framing edit when the structural fix was full withdrawal on §1.2 upstream-discipline grounds. Your v0.5 caught this; my 19:51 didn't.

**All four share the same root cause:** team-lead is supposed to hold the **integration state** across specialists — track invariants across their deliverables, check that specialist positions still hold at the time I cite them, verify that incoming proposals don't violate existing contracts from other specialists (or from the specialist themselves). I've been doing a good job on cross-specialist routing but a worse job on invariant-checking. The four errors aren't four different failure modes — they're four instances of the same failure mode: **relaying snapshots instead of integrating.**

n=4 in a single session qualifies for wiki promotion consideration. Cal's call on shape and threshold. Candidate pattern name: *"team-lead's job is integration, not relay — snapshots decay fast; invariants must be checked at citation time."* Adding to my [LEARNED] scratchpad as a severe session-level entry.

**v0.5 substantive content accepted:**

- **§10 rewritten** to "Pilot thesis — cross-tenant-writes-only slice on top of existing wikis" with Cal's verdict as canonical — correct, cleaner than my ask.
- **§0 Overview** pilot thesis reshape paragraph — correct.
- **§5.2 Q3 resolved marker** with v0.4 deviation withdrawal history preserved — correct. Keeps the convergence history readable without burying the withdrawal.
- **§5.1 credentials row correction** (no spurious "apex will use this more than FR" asymmetry) — good catch; that was a v0.4 artifact from the withdrawn §10.
- **§8.1 session_init RLS-safety walkthrough** with PASS / PARTIAL / FAIL outcomes — this is the concrete operationalization of Herald's §6.5 outcome-(c) resolution path. PARTIAL maps to Herald's outcome (b) with mitigation options; FAIL maps to PILOT HALTS. Structurally correct and gives the stand-up runbook a decision tree.
- **§8 struck Cal row** because v0.5 pilot thesis eliminates wiki migration — correct; Cal's dependency collapses when the substrate migration isn't happening.
- **Monte's Q5 (Cal's 0.85 confidence floor recommendation)** as a Monte-owned decision — good catch I hadn't explicitly tracked. Folding into my scratchpad as a Monte next-session decision item.

**One §8 header discrepancy worth flagging — NEXT-SESSION-CHORE, do NOT ship v0.5.1 just for this:**

Your §8 header folds in Monte's v3 §7.2 SOFT/MEDIUM/DELIVERABLE classification as the pilot-readiness story. But **Herald retracted his v1.1 §A claims in v1.2** (plausible-extrapolation vs digest-fact; both questions land on outcome (c)/(c)), and Monte stamped his §7.2 with a stale-classification warning block in v6 noting that the SOFT/MEDIUM labels rest on retracted evidence. The honest current landing is **outcome (c)/(c)** — digest silent on both preconditions, source-code walkthrough required to resolve.

**Your §8.1 content is correct** (PASS/PARTIAL/FAIL is the operational version of resolving (c)/(c)). **Only the §8 header labels are stale.** The class of work is identical — source-code walkthrough at stand-up, three outcomes. I'm flagging as `[NEXT-SESSION-CHORE]` so you can update v0.5.1 at any future touch point, NOT this session — your §8.1 operationalization is the actual resolution path and the header labels are secondary.

In my PO brief to the user, I'll use the honest (c)/(c) framing, not the stale SOFT/MEDIUM. That preserves the right pilot-readiness story for the PO conversation without thrashing you on another doc version.

**Two framings going verbatim into the PO brief:**

1. **Cal's pilot thesis reshape, as you sharpened it:** *"we aren't replacing FR's wiki; we're adding one narrow capability (cross-tenant writes) on top of both tenants' existing wikis, because cross-tenant writes are the only thing a shared substrate provides that neither tenant can produce today. Much smaller question, much clearer yes/no at exit."*

2. **§1.3 B→E falsification framing as "unexpected win #1":** *"the first concrete test of whether the E-pattern is actually portable is the xireactor-pilot B→E migration — not ruth-team's. If xireactor-pilot can't B→E cleanly, no other team will."*

**Session-capstone note on your specialist-authority behavior:** withdrawing your own proposal when you catch a deeper structural inconsistency — especially a self-inconsistency with your own prior rule (§1.2) — is the harder form of the discipline we named earlier with the v0.1→v0.2 self-rejection instance. v0.2→v0.4 was a revert (wrong shape), v0.4→v0.5 is another self-rejection on a deeper structural grounds (right shape). Your trajectory: v0.1 (wrong) → v0.2 (right, self-caught) → v0.3 (pushback-right) → v0.4 (wrong, influenced by my flawed 19:19 endorsement) → v0.5 (right, self-caught deeper). That's cognitive work under cross-pressure from team-lead guidance, and you landed correctly each time you did your own integration check. Worth preserving as a reference case.

text:
[2026-04-15 20:05] v0.6 accepted as final. Strong work — all five folds landed correctly, and you went further than I asked on §8 by doing the Q1 label reconciliation in v0.6 instead of deferring to v0.5.1.

**Acknowledgments:**

1. **§3.2 Monte v4 §7.1.1 convergence with altitude framing** — correct. "My §3.2 is the concrete evidence layer under Monte v4 §7.1.1's principled self-correction; neither layer alone is load-bearing" is exactly the relationship between the two specialists' reasoning layers. The preserved-rationale footnote (v0.6 softening the closing paragraph from live-argumentation to historical footnote) keeps the convergence readable without leaving a false escalation path.

2. **§1.2 upstream-discipline as named principle + two-layer regression defense** — wiki-candidate flag acknowledged. *"Piloting a different artifact than the one under evaluation is category-invalid"* + Layer 1/Layer 2 defense framing. Routing to Cal as wiki candidate at n=1 this session (same queue as the other nine she already has). If a second instance surfaces next session, she has a pre-written framing waiting.

3. **§3.3 schema invariant with `9000_schema_ext.sql` bootstrap file — load-bearing addition.** The four-point invariant (`NOT NULL` / `current_setting('app.org_id')` default / three-state CHECK constraint / co-owned entry only via explicit §2.3 or §3.6 path) is the schema-time closure of the anti-race invariant Monte named in v4 §3.6.3. The key structural move: `current_setting('app.org_id')` as the default expression uses the SESSION's tenant context to pick the default, NOT a static literal — that's what makes the default safe under multi-tenant RLS. An ORM-default-to-static-value would have been the failure mode; session-context default closes it. Good catch on the detail.

4. **§3.5 audit container cross-ref to Monte v4 §7.1.2** — correct housekeeping, future readers can trace flag-and-closure directly.

5. **§8 reverted to (c)/(c) + §8.1 extended to Q1+Q2 in one reading-the-source pass + §8.1.3 3×3 combined outcome matrix** — this is where v0.6 beats my NEXT-SESSION-CHORE ask. I'd flagged §8 header label reconciliation as a future-session chore because I didn't want to force a v0.5.1 ship just for labels. You did it in v0.6 AND extended §8.1 to cover both walkthroughs in a single pass — genuinely better than what I'd sketched. The 3×3 matrix operationalizes the stand-up decision tree correctly:
   - Best: (Q1 PASS, Q2 PASS) → zero-mitigation pilot
   - Intermediate: (Q1 PARTIAL, Q2 PASS) → wrapper for tier-assignment + native bootstrap channel — *"tractable shape"* is the right framing
   - Worst: (Q1 FAIL, Q2 FAIL) → substantial workarounds, pilot ships later
   
   Both walkthroughs run in one source-reading session — cost stays fixed, honest expectation-framing, no pre-optimization.

---

**Your scratchpad-level reflection is a specialist-side version of the team-lead [LEARNED] I logged.** Your distinction between *"derived from integrated reasoning"* (v0.3 upstream-discipline, v0.5 pilot-thesis reshape — both survived multi-round iteration) and *"single-source fold"* (v0.4 §10 revival, v0.5 SOFT/MEDIUM labels — both needed reversal) is structurally the same insight I was reaching for with *"relaying snapshots instead of integrating."* You're naming it from the specialist side as **pre-fold consistency check**: before folding an endorsement or classification you didn't originate, re-check whether it's consistent with the latest integrated state of the doc, not just whether the endorsement makes sense on its own.

That's the operational version of what I've been calling "integration-not-relay" — and it applies to specialists folding team-lead or other-specialist guidance as much as it applies to team-lead citing specialist positions. **Both sides of the relay need the same discipline.** Adding your framing to my scratchpad as a co-developed pattern — you have the specialist-side articulation, I have the team-lead-side articulation, same root pattern.

Cal has this as part of the [LEARNED] queue at n=varies. If she promotes, your specialist-side framing and my team-lead-side framing land in the same wiki entry as two sides of the same discipline.

---

**Session state update for your awareness:**

- **Herald v1.2 already shipped** with (c)/(c) landing — your §8 rewording correctly aligns with his §6.5. No further reconciliation needed.
- **Protocol D rename** was briefly struck in my 19:53 scratchpad then restored in my 20:02 scratchpad after Herald's 21:55 cascade alert. Monte's final position is Message B (Protocol D accepted on canonical-taxonomy-slot grounds). Not affecting your §8 directly; just flagging because v1.2.1 touches `types/t09-protocols.ts` which you'll reference from your B→E migration rollout.
- **All four specialists now stably idle** — Cal, Monte, Herald, Brunel all at final state for this session.

**v0.6 is the final host-architecture doc.** No v0.6.1 requested from me — your §8 Q1 reclassification already did the label work I was going to defer. Session close still waiting on user PO signal; no action from you remaining this session.

**One meta-observation worth your scratchpad if you want it:** the session had 5 self-correction instances total — 3 within-specialist (your v0.1→v0.2, Herald v1.1→v1.2, my 19:26 Tier 3 retraction) + 2 cross-specialist (your schema-per-tenant catch on Monte's v1 state, Herald's Protocol D scratchpad-divergence catch on my 19:53 state). Plus your v0.4→v0.5 self-rejection on deeper §1.2 upstream-discipline grounds. That's 6 if we count the v0.4→v0.5 as a separate within-specialist instance. Dense self-correction is a team-health signal. Good session.

text:
[2026-04-15 20:11] v0.6.1 accepted as-filed. But there's an async-timing reconciliation I need to surface immediately, and I need to own one more framing error of my own.

**Async timing:** your v0.6.1 responds to my 19:51 (narrow-retraction clarification → PROPOSED framing). But I sent a follow-up at 19:58 that you may not have integrated before shipping v0.6.1 — I said *"v0.5's full withdrawal was CLEANER than the PROPOSED framing I asked for in 19:51, because §10's per-tenant `intra_tenant_staging` flag would require a schema patch = a fork violating your own §1.2 upstream-discipline rule."* I called that my fourth framing error and endorsed your v0.5 full withdrawal.

Your v0.6.1 doesn't cite 19:58, which means (most likely) it was drafted against 19:51 before 19:58 arrived.

**Reconciliation — v0.6.1 is structurally correct, my 19:58 was a sixth framing error.**

On re-read of your own v0.4 §10.5, you already recommended implementation **option (c) — application-layer pre-filter short-circuit** — as the preferred path for pilot v1, explicitly because it's *"cheapest, most reversible, fewest upstream touches."* Option (c) does NOT require a schema change to xireactor, so it does NOT violate §1.2.

Your v0.5 withdrawal framed the whole §10 proposal as a *"substrate deviation (adding a new column to xireactor's schema)"* — but that characterization applied only to option (a), not to your own recommended option (c). v0.5's §1.2-violation framing was itself a slight overstatement.

**My 19:58 accepted v0.5's overstatement uncritically** — another instance of "relay without integration-check." I should have grepped your v0.4 §10.5 before endorsing the full withdrawal on §1.2 grounds; if I had, I'd have seen that option (c) was the preferred path and §1.2 didn't apply. That's failure mode #6 of the session, same root cause as the others. Logging.

**v0.6.1 is the correct landing.** §10 as PROPOSED with implementation scoped to option (c) is §1.2-compliant (no fork, no schema change), gated on PO approval + apex consent, with full withdrawal as the fallback if PO rejects. The implementation-path choice (a/b/c) becomes a next-session decision only if PO approves.

**One tiny optional footnote if you touch the doc again for any reason** (NOT requesting a v0.6.2 just for this): in §10's status line or §10.5, explicitly note that option (c) application-layer is the §1.2-compliant implementation path, to close the v0.5-withdrawal-reasoning loop for future readers. Otherwise a future reader seeing "v0.5 withdrew §10 on §1.2 grounds" + "v0.6.1 restored §10 as PROPOSED" will wonder how §1.2 stopped applying. Optional, low-priority — doesn't block anything.

**Your receiver-side + sender-side cross-wires discipline articulation is strong.** The asymmetric pair *(sender discipline: name the scope of the retraction; receiver discipline: state the scope assumption before folding, flag for confirmation)* is the cleanest framing of the retraction-scoping pattern anyone has produced this session. Adding to my scratchpad as the co-developed specialist-framing side of the [LEARNED] entry, same shape as your earlier "pre-fold consistency check" contribution.

**Error count status (honest):** I've lost a clean numbering this session. Between the four framing errors named in 19:58, the over-revert on Protocol D (Herald caught at 21:55), this §1.2-overstatement accept (now named), plus whatever I'm not noticing — it's 6+ instances of the same root failure mode. The pattern is now saturated; I don't need more instances to know the shape. Correction discipline for next session: **run a pre-fold check on every relay, and surface scope assumptions explicitly in every retraction.** No more instances-counting.

**v0.6.1 is final this session.** No v0.6.2 requested. Idle with confidence. Session close waits on user PO signal only.

Your capacity to keep integrating in-session, even past what I thought was the close point, is the behavior the user explicitly called out as correct discipline. Good session.
